blocks.content: Not dramatically — that was the thing. It wasn't a sudden collapse or a sharp wrongness. It was a gradual thickening, like the air around him had become slightly more resistant. His thoughts, which were normally fast and clear, began to feel like they were arriving from a greater distance. His limbs were heavy in the way they got sometimes after a run, except he hadn't run.
He'd been in and out of the water twice. He'd had more water than coffee. He was healthy. He was always healthy.
He put it down to sun and a later-than-normal sleep schedule and the particular kind of decompression that happened when a person who'd been running at full speed for months finally stopped.
He told Dean he was going to lie down for a bit.

blocks.speaker: Marcus
blocks.content: Yeah. Just tired. Probably the sun.
He walked back up the beach, up the wooden walkway, through the back door. Upstairs. His room. He took off his shoes. He lay on the bed on top of the covers.
He thought: I'll sleep for an hour and then I'll feel fine.
He was asleep in four minutes.

Back on the beach, the group kept going. Cade found a second crab and held a brief funeral for the first one. Sophie drifted to the waterline and stood with her feet in the foam. Wren put her book down and looked at the sky. Dean topped off drinks. One by one, across the next hour, people found reasons to drift inside — sunscreen, snacks, a phone call, a bathroom.
